summary:
The Erligang culture was an early Bronze Age Chinese archaeological culture centered at the site of Erligang near Zhengzhou in Henan province, dated approximately 1510–1460 BC. It represents one of the earliest phases of the [[event:55|Shang dynasty]] and is associated with one of the most impressive urban sites of ancient China. The city at Zhengzhou, often identified with the Shang capital Bo, was enclosed by enormous rammed-earth walls measuring approximately 7 kilometers in perimeter and reaching heights of several meters, enclosing an area of roughly 3 square kilometers — one of the largest Bronze Age cities in the world at the time of its construction.

Erligang bronze casting was already highly sophisticated, producing large ritual vessels in shapes that anticipate the classical Shang bronze tradition of the later [[event:1841|Yin Xu]] period. The city contained dedicated bronze-casting workshops that were among the largest known metallurgical facilities of the ancient world, capable of producing vessels of several hundred kilograms. This industrial-scale bronze production required centralized control over the supply of copper and tin from distant ore sources and was a direct expression of royal power.

Erligang cultural influence extended far beyond the Zhengzhou core, with Erligang-style bronzes and architectural features appearing at sites across a vast area of northern, central, and southern China. This distribution suggests that the early Shang state exercised political and economic influence over a large portion of the Yellow River basin and beyond. The Erligang phase ended around 1400 BC, when the capital shifted northward to Anyang, marking the transition to the better-documented late Shang period.
